# Your job & prices and the EU — research dossier (draft)

**Topic:** How would EU membership affect jobs, wages, and the broader cost of living (beyond food)?
**Chapter:** "Your job & prices" (chapter 3)
**Status:** From one deep-research pass — 18 claims verified before a session limit cut off verification + synthesis. Auto-generated overnight; needs Gísli review + Icelandic write-up.
**Last updated:** 2026-06-17

> Confidence flags: ✅ verified (multi-vote) · ⚠️ unverified (verifier abstained on session limit — NOT refuted) · ❌ refuted (do not publish).

---

## BOTTOM LINE

For **jobs and the labour market, EU membership changes little** — free movement of workers, the
single market, and EU competition/State-aid rules **already apply to Iceland via the EEA.** The real
deltas are financial: the **euro could lower the cost of borrowing** (for firms and the state) and a
more integrated financial sector *could* trim fees/premiums over time. Iceland already has very high
wages **and** very high prices (partly from domestic market concentration) — and not all of that is
something EU membership would fix.

---

## TODAY (already true under the EEA)
- ✅ **Free movement of workers is already in force** — citizens of all 30 EEA countries can live,
  work, set up business and study across the EEA; non-discrimination by nationality. In 2023, **11.8%**
  of Iceland's workforce was from other EU states, **7.7%** third-country, 80.1% Icelandic. (govt.is;
  EFTA; EURES)
- ✅ Migration into Iceland rose **after it opened its labour market via the EEA** (not EU membership),
  and tracks the business cycle. (OECD 2017)
- ✅ **EU competition + State-aid rules already adopted** through the EEA — not contingent on
  membership. (European Parliament 2026 briefing)
- ✅ Iceland is **NOT** in: economic & monetary union (euro), customs union, common trade policy, or
  EU taxation. (European Parliament 2026)
- ✅ **High wages:** 2023 gross avg earnings €6,184/mo vs €3,417 EU27 (~1.8×); net €4,490 vs €2,351.
  (EURES; net figure 2-1)
- ✅ **High prices, partly from concentration:** in 2024 Iceland had the **highest prices of 36
  European countries** for footwear, household appliances, transport services, and alcohol/tobacco;
  overall price level above the EU "especially in services," with transport/communication "markedly
  higher … possibly resulting from market concentration." (Eurostat 2024; OECD Economic Survey 2025)

## AS A MEMBER (what changes)
- ✅ **Labour market: little changes** — the four freedoms already apply via EEA.
- ✅ **Cheaper external financing with the euro:** euro adopters saw cost-of-capital fall **25–100
  basis points**; staying out means continuing to pay an exchange-rate risk premium. (Icelandic govt
  currency-options report)
- ✅ **More competition / efficient financial sector** *could* mean less costly intermediation, lower
  insurance & pension premiums, and over time higher productivity/incomes. (govt currency-options
  report — framed as potential, not certainty)

## GAINS
- ✅ **Lower cost of borrowing** for businesses and the state via the euro (25–100 bps). (govt report)
- ✅ **Potential lower financial-services fees & premiums** from deeper integration/competition. (govt report)
- ✅ Single-market membership historically **raised EU GDP 3–4% and created 3.6M jobs** since 1993 —
  though Iceland already gets most single-market access via the EEA. (European Commission, 2-0)

## LOSSES / COSTS
- ✅ **Loss of the currency safety valve:** Iceland's unit labour costs grew **5.6%** (2016–25, ~2×
  Nordic neighbours) and real wages **+2.4%/yr** (vs 0.6–0.8% in DK/NO/FI) — "a consistent loss of
  international competitiveness." Outside the euro, devaluation absorbs that; inside, it can't.
  (govt currency-options report) — ties to the verðtrygging/króna trade-off in chapter 1.
- ⚠️ **High prices are partly domestic** (market concentration in services/transport) — not all
  fixable by EU membership; some restrictive regulation sits in areas the EEA doesn't touch. *(the
  "438 competition restrictions" detail was inconclusive 1-0 — re-check.)*

## UNCERTAIN / HONEST UNKNOWNS
- ⚠️ **Does EU competition actually lower Icelandic consumer prices?** The direct "competition lowers
  prices" + EU price-concentration evidence (e.g. extra mobile operator → 7–9% lower prices;
  concentrated mortgage markets → higher rates) were **abstained on the session limit — unverified,
  re-check** before using.
- ⚠️ Whether EU price levels **converge or diverge** over time (abstained — unverified).
- Because most labour/competition effects are **already realized via the EEA**, membership's marginal
  jobs/wages impact is genuinely modest and hard to pin down.

## DO NOT PUBLISH (refuted)
- ❌ "Icelandic labour productivity is ~11% below OECD average / lowest in the Nordics, growth 0.4%/yr"
  — **refuted 0-3.**

## KEY SOURCES
**Primary:** Icelandic govt *Skýrsla um valkosti Íslands í gjaldmiðlamálum* (currency-options report);
European Parliament 2026 Iceland briefing; EURES Iceland labour-market info; Eurostat comparative price
levels 2024; OECD Economic Survey Iceland 2025; OECD 2017 labour-market report; EFTA; European
Commission single-market pages. **Secondary:** UK in a Changing Europe, Nordisk Post.
